summary: Gruber responds to criticism that iPad users are losing out by not having Flash support, arguing that short-term pain is necessary for long-term web health. He contends that Flash Player doesn't work well on any mobile platform, pointing to Android's poor implementation as evidence. He argues that as long as every browser supports Flash, websites will never migrate away from it, making iOS's lack of Flash a necessary catalyst for change. He acknowledges iPad users suffer with missing content now, but maintains this is the only path to a web free from dependence on a proprietary, single-company-controlled platform.
key_points: Flash Player performs poorly on Android and isn't available on other mobile platforms, undermining the assumption Apple could make it work well on iOS
Apple doesn't have Flash source code, and even if they did, there's no reason to think Apple engineers could optimize it better than Adobe's own team
Flash will never decline as a de facto web standard as long as every browser supports it — someone has to break the cycle
The short-term cost to iPad users (missing content) is the unavoidable price of moving the web toward open standards
Apple's self-interest in promoting HTML5 over Flash happens to align with the public interest in open web technologies
money_quotes: But let's assume possibility (a), that Apple could 'just make it work'. But then what? We're stuck with Flash — a proprietary closed-source platform controlled by a single company — as a de facto web standard forever?
Flash is never going to decrease in popularity so long as all web browsers support it.
If no one releases a popular web browsing platform that lacks Flash support, then web sites that already publish Flash content are never going to move away from it.
I think the web would be a far better place without Flash, or, at least, with Flash relegated to a position like that of Java applets: there if you want it, but not a major foundation.
In the short term, iPad users suffer with empty white squares and missing content. There's no other way to get there from here, though.
I'm not arguing that Apple is selflessly acting in the interest of the public. I'm arguing that Apple's self interest is aligned with the interest of the public with regard to Flash.
key_insight: The only way to free the web from dependence on Flash as a de facto standard is for a major platform to refuse to support it, accepting short-term user pain as the necessary cost of long-term openness.
